title: Ossobuco alla milanese
subtitle: Braised beef shin in white wine with gremolata
excerpt: Cross-cut beef shin braised in white wine, vegetables, and beef stock until the meat falls from the bone and the marrow can be spooned from the hollow centre. Finished with gremolata (lemon zest, garlic, and parsley) scattered over at the table to cut through the richness.
sourceNotes: Ossobuco alla milanese (braised hollow bone, Milanese-style) is the signature dish of Lombardy and Milan, documented from the nineteenth century. The traditional version uses veal shin; this recipe uses beef shin, which is widely available and produces an equally fine braise. The gremolata (lemon zest, garlic, and flat parsley) is the classical garnish: it is added at the table, not cooked, and is not optional. Traditionally served with risotto alla milanese (saffron risotto) or plain polenta.

content.text: The shin pieces must be tied with kitchen string before browning, the string holds the meat to the bone as the collagen softens during braising, keeping the portions intact. Without it, the meat can slide off and disintegrate. Tie each piece around its circumference like a parcel.

The braise goes into the oven, not stays on the hob, oven heat surrounds the pot evenly and maintains the gentle, low simmer that prevents the collagen from tightening and the meat from drying. Two hours at 160°C fan produces meat that falls from the bone while still holding its shape; more than 2.5 hours and it begins to shred.

items.name: Beef shin
items.prepNote: cross-cut, ~4 cm thick, tied around the circumference with kitchen string

items.name: Garlic
items.prepNote: 2 for the braise (sliced), 1 for the gremolata (minced)

content.text: Preheat the oven to 160°C fan. Season the {{beef-shin}} pieces and dust lightly with {{plain-flour}}. Heat the {{olive-oil}} and {{unsalted-butter}} in a wide casserole over high heat. Brown the shin pieces for 3 minutes per side until deeply coloured. Remove. Reduce the heat to medium. Add the {{onion}}, {{carrot}}, and {{celery}} and cook for 8 minutes until softened. Add 2 sliced cloves of {{garlic}} and cook for 1 minute. Pour in the {{dry-white-wine}} and let it bubble for 2 minutes, scraping the base. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}}, {{stock-beef}}, and {{bay-leaves}}.

Return the shin pieces to the casserole, standing upright so the marrow stays in the bone. The liquid should come about two-thirds up the shin. Cover with a tight-fitting lid and braise in the oven for 2 hours until the meat is very tender and beginning to pull from the bone. Rest for 10 minutes. Remove the string and the bay leaves.

Combine the {{parsley-flat}}, {{lemon}} zest, and minced {{garlic}} for the gremolata. Plate the ossobuco with risotto or polenta. Spoon the braising sauce over and scatter the gremolata over the top at the table.

items.fix: Always tie the pieces before browning; check tenderness at 90 minutes. It should be yielding but intact
items.cause: String not tied before braising, or braised too long
items.symptom: Meat has fallen off the bone and disintegrated

items.fix: Remove the lid for the last 20 minutes of braising; or reduce the sauce separately after removing the meat
items.cause: Too much liquid, or lid not tight enough allowing steam to escape

items.fix: Grate only the yellow zest; use just one small garlic clove, the gremolata should be fragrant, not pungent
items.cause: Lemon pith included, or too much raw garlic

items.fix: Stand the pieces upright (hollow side up) so the marrow has to melt out downwards against gravity; keep the braise at a low simmer
items.cause: Pieces not standing upright, or temperature too high
items.symptom: Marrow has run out of the bone during braising

content.text: Ossobuco alla milanese is the signature dish of Milan. The city's cooking is serious and understated and trusts technique over decoration. The name means 'hollow bone'. The osso is the shin cross-cut. The buco is the marrow cavity at the centre. The marrow is eaten with a narrow spoon, straight from the bone. That alone is a good reason to order it. Gremolata is specific to the Milanese version. It cuts through the rich braise, and the dish is not complete without it.
